coredata.dc:title: Carbon-Nanodots modified glassy carbon electrode for the electroanalysis of selenium in water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Idris, Azeez O
Orimolade, Benjamin O
Mafa, Potlako J.
Kuvarega, Alex T.
Feleni, Usisipho
Mamba, Bhekie B.
coredata.dc:description: We report a simple and cheaper method for the electrochemical detection of Se(IV) using carbon nanodots (CNDTs) prepared from oat. The carbon nanodots were synthesised by a green and facile approach and characterised using scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy. The CNDT was used to fabricate an electrochemical sensor for the quantification of Se(IV) in water. The modification of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with carbon nanodots led to an increase in the electroactive surface area of the electrode, which enhances the redox current peak of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– in comparison to the bare GCE. Using the square wave voltammetry, the detection limit and quantification limit of 0.05 and 0.167 ppb were obtained under the optimised parameters using deposition potential of −200 mV, 0.1 M HNO3 electrolyte, electrodeposition time of 60 s, and pH 1. The results further revealed that the GCE-CNDT was not susceptible to many interfering cations except Cu(II) and Pb(II) and Fe(II). The sensor fabrication involves a one-step electrode modification and was used to detect Se(IV) in a real water sample and the result obtained is in agreement with the inductively coupled plasma technique. Overall, the electrode offers a cheap, fast and sensitive way of detecting selenium in environmental matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Square wave voltammetry
Nanomaterials
Selenium
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interrogating the morphology of carbon nanodots
Optimisation: The effect of pH, supporting electrolyte, deposition potential and deposition time
Interference, stability, reproducibility studies and exploring the applicability of GCE-CNDTs in a real water sample
Quantification of selenium in a real water sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The SEM image of carbon nanodots at 50 µm magnification b) TEM image of carbon nanodots at 50 nm. c) Size distributions of the carbon nanodots.
(a) FTIR spectra of carbon nanodots, (b) XRD diffraction pattern of carbon nanodots, and (c) Raman spectra of the CNDTs d) UV–Visible DRS spectrum of carbon nanodots.

(a) CV of bare GCE and GCE-CNDTs in a solution containing ferrocyanide (b) CV of different scan rates of an electrochemical sensor (GCE-CNDTs) inset: a plot of current vs square root of scan rates, Potential vs Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) and (c) EIS of bare GCE and GCE-CNDTs.
Optimisation parameters of Se(IV) detection (a) pH, (b) supporting electrolytes, (c) electrodeposition potential, and (d) electrodeposition time.

(a) The overlay of 1000 ppb solution of Se(IV) on bare GCE and GCE-CNDTs electrodes using SWASV and (b) SWASV responses for the detection of Se(IV) on GCE-CNDTs with the calibration graph (inset), where n = 3.
(a) The interference study for the detection of Se(IV) in water, (b) and (c) Stability and reproducibility results for the detection of 1000 ppb Se(IV).
Comparing the reports on the detection limits of Se(IV) using different electrochemical sensors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: BiFe-Bismuth film electrode, AC-Activated Carbon, OSWV- Osteryoung Square Wave Voltammetry, HMDE-Hanging mercury electrode, CCSV-Catalytic Cathodic Stripping Voltammetry, AuNPs- gold nanoparticles, MGUA-Micro gold ultramicroelectrode array, rGO-reduced graphene oxide, GUME-Gold ultra-micro electrode, MEA- microband electrode array, GCE-AuNC@FG- Glassy Carbon Electrode Gold Nanocages/Fluorinated Graphene Nanocomposite,

head.ce:title: Carbon-Nanodots modified glassy carbon electrode for the electroanalysis of selenium in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Private Bag X6, Florida Science Campus, 1709 Johannesburg, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS)
College of Science, Engineering and Technology
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Private Bag X6, Florida Science Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Private Bag X6, Florida Science Campus, 1709 Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a simple and cheaper method for the electrochemical detection of Se(IV) using carbon nanodots (CNDTs) prepared from oat. The carbon nanodots were synthesised by a green and facile approach and characterised using scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy. The CNDT was used to fabricate an electrochemical sensor for the quantification of Se(IV) in water. The modification of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with carbon nanodots led to an increase in the electroactive surface area of the electrode, which enhances the redox current peak of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– in comparison to the bare GCE. Using the square wave voltammetry, the detection limit and quantification limit of 0.05 and 0.167 ppb were obtained under the optimised parameters using deposition potential of −200 mV, 0.1 M HNO3 electrolyte, electrodeposition time of 60 s, and pH 1. The results further revealed that the GCE-CNDT was not susceptible to many interfering cations except Cu(II) and Pb(II) and Fe(II). The sensor fabrication involves a one-step electrode modification and was used to detect Se(IV) in a real water sample and the result obtained is in agreement with the inductively coupled plasma technique. Overall, the electrode offers a cheap, fast and sensitive way of detecting selenium in environmental matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Selenium is a microelement that plays a critical role in the health of humans, plants and animals when consumed in the appropriate amount [1]. Selenium is a naturally occurring element found in various metalloids, and it is found in water, earth crust and the atmosphere at low concentrations [2]. It plays an important role in the body by preventing cancerous diseases, for instance, selenoproteins such as thioredoxin reductases, iodothyroxine deiodinases, and gluthathione peroxidases (GSHPx) help protect against oxidative stress, and in the reduction of intramolecular disulfide bonds [3]. Chronic exposure to selenium can cause selenosis which is mainly due to long-term intake of selenium in contaminated drinking water [3].

Selenium deficiency has caused various diseases such as cancer, muscular dystrophy, osteoarthropathy, heart diseases, multiple sclerosis and reproductive disorder. On the other hand, excessive intake of selenium has been linked to several health issues including nerve damage, hair loss, gastrointestinal upsets, garlic breath odour and white blotchy nails [4]. Hence, it is important to monitor the concentrations of selenium in the body and our environment. Recently, the quantification of selenium has attracted the strong interest from water technologists, electrochemists, and environmentalists due to its presence in water above the permissible limits by the World Health Organisation. Hence, the World Health Organization has set a limit of 10 ppb for selenium in drinking water [5].

Human activities such as mining, phosphate deposits, processing of base metal, pharmaceuticals, paint, electronics, production of shampoo and detergents have contributed significantly to the concentration of selenium in the environment [6]. Due to the environmental impact and toxicity of selenium, different techniques such as spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma and electrochemistry methods have been used to quantify selenium in various matrices [7–9]. Although significant progress has been reported in the use of spectroscopy and inductively coupled plasma techniques for the quantification of selenium, both techniques suffered several limitations such as long analysis time, extensive sample preparation, require sophisticated personnel and the instruments cannot be used on-site [10].

On the other hand, electrochemical techniques have gained remarkable attention due to the portability of the instrument, low cost, ease of sample preparation, and fast analysis time [11]. Bertolino et al. employed square wave anodic stripping voltammetry to quantify selenium in natural water using activated carbon and a detection limit of 0.004 ppb were obtained within the concentration range of 0.001 to 20 ppb [12]. In another report, differential pulse adsorptive stripping voltammetry was used to detect selenium on bismuth film electrode, using p-amino benzene and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide as complexing agents [13]. Rhodium was used to modify glassy carbon electrodes for the detection of selenium using catalytic cathodic stripping voltammetry, the catalytic properties of rhodium facilitated the electrochemical detection of selenium at a low detection limit [14].

For signal enhancement, nanomaterials have been used as excellent modifiers for the electrochemical detection of various heavy metals [13–14]. Carbon nanodots are a new class of nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, they are rising stars in the nanocarbon family, because of their benign, abundant and low-cost nature [16,17]. Carbon nanodots (CNDTs) are discrete, quasi-spherical particles [18]. They possess an sp2 conjugated core and consist of suitable oxygen content in form of different functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde groups [19]. CNDTs are unique nanomaterials because of their interesting analytical properties such as optical, chemical, low cost, low cytotoxicity, high surface area, excellent biocompatibility, ease of functionalisation, resistance to photo-bleaching, catalytic and biological properties [14–15]. These properties have significantly contributed to the application of CNDTs for various applications including heavy metal detection [22–23], medical diagnosis [21–22], energy [23–27], catalysis, bio-imaging, light-emitting diode (LED), energy storage, and drug delivery [28–32]. Thus, it is important to consider the application of carbon nanodots as potential electrochemical nanowires for the detection of Se(IV) in water. It is important to highlight that the electrostatic attraction between the negative charge of the carbon nanodots and the positive charge of the selenium (IV) allured us towards using this nanomaterial for the quantification of Se(IV) in water.

Considering this, we report for the first time the application of carbon nanodots for the development of an electrochemical sensor for the detection of selenium (IV) in water. In this work, a low cost, green, and facile approach were employed for the synthesis of carbon nanodots, which was subsequently utilised for the first time as a platform for the electrochemical detection of selenium. The novelty of this work is the ease of the sensor development, which involve a one-step approach and the large surface area of the modifier, which aided the electron communication of the sensor.

K2HPO4, KH2PO4, KCl, H2SO4, Na2SeO3, HNO3, HCl, and ethanol were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals for these experiments were used as obtained and were all prepared in deionised water. All electrochemical experiments were done on a Metrohm PGSTAT 302 N electrochemical station. The instrument is attached to a three-electrode system, including a working electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE), and reference electrode (RE). The counter electrode is platinum wire; a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as a working electrode and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as a reference electrode. Ultra-pure argon gas was used to degas the electrochemical cells for approximately 10 min before performing any electrochemical experiment. The following techniques/tools were used for the characterisation– FTIR (Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100, USA) using KBr pellet technique, XRD using Rigaku SmartLab x-ray diffractometer (USA). Electron micrographs were taken with SEM (TESCAN, Vega 3 XMU, Czech Republic) and TEM (JEOL 2100 HRTEM 200 V, Japan). Raman spectrum was obtained on Raman Microscope (PerkinElmer RamanMicro 200 USA) with x50 objective.

Carbon nanodots were synthesised according to the method reported in the literature with slight modification [33,34]. Briefly, 10 g mass of oats was weighed, crushed, and pyrolyzed at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. The oats changed from white to black after cooling at ambient temperature, the resulting substance was crushed into fine powder to reduce the size of the material. Thereafter, it was dispersed in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 7800 rpm repeatedly to remove larger particles. The carbon nanodot aqueous suspension was filtered and dried in an oven for 24 h at 80 ˚C.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were used to investigate the effect of modifying the glassy carbon electrode with carbon nanodots using 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– prepared in 0.1 M KCl as a redox probe. The CV electroanalytical experiment was carried out by applying a potential from −0.3 to 0.8 V, E-step of 10 mV, the scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and a current range of 1 µA. The electroanalysis of Se(IV) was performed using SWV techniques in the potential range of −950 and + 950 mV; and parameters such as pH, electrolyte, electrodeposition potential, and electrodeposition time were optimised.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interrogating the morphology of carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM and TEM were employed to investigate the morphological features of the synthesised nanodots. A porous nanostructure was observed from the SEM image of the CNDTs as shown in Fig. 1 (a). An oval/spherical shape carbon nanodots were obtained from the TEM image in Fig. 1(b). The size distribution of the carbon nanodots was calculated to have an average particle size of 7 nm (Fig. 1(c)), this was smaller than the report documented by Shi et al. modification [33]. The reason for this observation was due to the crushing of the starting material before placing it in the muffle furnace.

The FTIR spectrum of carbon nanodots shown in Fig. 2 (a) gave peaks at 3430, 2923, and 1620 cm−1, which correspond to the –OH vibration of water molecules, aliphatic (C–H) stretching and carbonyl (C = O) stretching vibration, respectively. Besides, the peaks located at 1391 and 1038 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric carboxylate stretch and C–O bonds of ethers, carboxylic acids and polysaccharides [20–21]. The XRD diffractogram of carbon nanodot is depicted in Fig. 2(b) with the diffraction peak at 2Θ = 24.63°, which corresponds to graphite (002) and indicates the presence of graphitic materials. Another diffraction peak at 2Θ = 42.64° indexed to (111) lattice plane was also observed and a similar observation has been reported in the literature [36]. The low-intensity peak at 42.64° corresponds to the diamond phase in the carbon nanodots. The crystallite size of the carbon nanodot was calculated using the Debye-Scherer equation as shown in equation (1):
(1)
L
α
=
4
K
λ
β
C
o
s
θ

Where
L α
represents the size of graphite crystallite, λ is the wavelength (1.52 Å), K is Scherer constant (0.9), β is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity (FWHM) corresponding peak (002) in radian units and θ is diffraction angle. The crystalline size was calculated to be ∼ 7 nm using equation (1). The obtained size agrees with the TEM average size of the nanodots. Raman spectrum of the carbon nanodots is indicated in Fig. 2(c). Two broad peaks were observed at ∼1342 and ∼ 1571 cm−1 corresponding to graphite‘s D and G bands. The G-band corresponds to the first-order scattering of the E2g mode observed for sp2 domains and the D band is attributed to the A1g mode usually observed for disordered graphite [35]. The D band at 1342 cm−1 in CNDTs indicate the presence of amorphous carbon and surface defects while the high intensity of 1571 cm−1 is assigned to the graphite phonon mode often associated with graphite, suggesting that the synthesised carbon nanodots were composed of crystalline graphitic carbon. The intensity of the D and G band (ID/IG) is a measurement of the disorder extent and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon [21]. The ratio of (D and G peaks) which is a measure of disorder in the crystal structure is found to be 0.88. This ratio reveals a high degree of disorder in the carbon sample.

The optical property of the prepared carbon nanodots (CNDTs) was investigated by recording its absorption spectrum through UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the CNDTs displayed a broad absorption band with decreasing intensity ranging from around 270 nm – 500 nm which resulted from the π – π* transition of CNDTs as established by previous reports [37–38]. This broad absorption band is ideal for the photoluminescent emission of the CNDTs under excitation from UV light to red light. Additionally, the absorption edge of the CNDTs is within the visible range which suggests the suitability of the material for visible light activity.

The cyclic voltammetry was used to interrogate the effect of modifying the bare glassy carbon electrode with carbon nanodots in a solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– prepared in 0.1 M KCl, using a scan rate of 50 mV/s as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The bare GCE had a pair of well-defined quasi-reversible peaks, and a significant enhancement in peak current signal was observed on modifying GCE with carbon nanodots, this electrode was labelled as GCE-CNDTs. The increase in peak current signal is attributed to an increase in the surface area of the electrode and the promotion of electron transfer on the electrode surface. The electroactive surface area of the electrode increased from 0.2072 cm2 for bare GCE to 0.5222 cm2 for GCE-CNDTs using Randle s Sevcik equation [39].
(2)
I
p
a
=
±
O
. 436
n
F A r e a l
C
√
n
F
D
V
R
T

The Ipa represents the anodic peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred in the redox event, Aff is the effective area, D is the diffusion coefficient, V is the scan rate, and C0 is the concentration of the redox probe.

The effect of varying scan rate on the performance of the electrochemical sensor (GCE-CNDTs) was interrogated in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– solution prepared in 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte. This was done to determine the kinetics and obtain information on the sensor stability. The results indicated a direct relationship between scan rate increase and enhanced anodic peak current. There was no significant shift in the anodic and cathodic peak currents as the scan rate increased. This observation can be used as an indicator to denote the stability of the electrochemical sensor, while leaching would have caused a major shift in potential. A plot of anodic peak current versus square root of scan rates displayed a good linear relationship indicating that the electrochemical kinetics is a diffusion-controlled process (Fig. 3(b) and inset). The linear regression equation obtained for the scan rate was I (µA) = 126.345X + 466.54, with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9964. The bare and GCE-CNDTs were characterised with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– prepared in 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte. The linear part at lower frequencies represents the diffusion process and the semi-circle diameter is the electron transfer resistance (Rct). The bare GCE displayed a well-defined semi-circle while there was an obvious reduction in the semi-circle for the modified electrode Fig. 3(c), this observation reveals that CNDTs are excellent nanowires that promote electron transport on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation: The effect of pH, supporting electrolyte, deposition potential and deposition time
ce:section.ce:para: Before the application of the proposed electrochemical sensor for the quantification of selenium, the pH, supporting electrolytes, deposition potential, and time were investigated to obtain the best electroanalytical condition for Se(IV) sensing. The effect of pH on the detection of selenium was investigated at pH of 1, 3, 7, 9, and 13 as depicted in Fig. 4 (a). It is important to carry out this analysis because the ionic states of metals are greatly affected by the proton environment (pH). For this study, pH 1 was used because it gave the highest current signal in comparison to other pH values. Similarly, various supporting electrolytes such as HNO3, HCl, H2SO4, KCl, NaOH and phosphate buffer solution were used to prepare Se(IV) and the result revealed that 0.1 M HNO3 was suitable for the stripping of Se(IV) because it gave the highest peak current signal (Fig. 4(b)). This shows that Se(IV) can best be detected in a highly acidic environment by the prepared sensor. The electrodeposition potential of −200 mV and electrodeposition time of 60 s was chosen as optimisation parameters for the pre-concentration step (Fig. 4(c-d)).

Using the optimised experimental conditions, the GCE-CNDTs electrodes gave a remarkable enhancement in peak current signal than the bare GCE during the stripping of 1000 ppb selenium (Fig. 5 (a)). This improvement is due to the improved surface area of the GCE-CNDTs, which promote electron communication on the electrode surface. The SWV of various Se(IV) concentrations were analysed from 1 ppb to 1000 ppb using the optimised parameters. The anodic peak current increased proportionately with the selenium concentrations (Fig. 5(b)). The linear regression equation of y = 0.08964x + 87.1272, a detection limit of 0.05 ppb, and a quantification limit of 0.167 ppb were obtained with an R2 value of 0.986. The detection limit (DL) and quantification limit (QL) were calculated using equations (3) and (4):
(3)
D
L
=
3
S
B
m
(4)
Q
L
=
10
S
B
m

Here, SB, and m are the standard deviation of the blank and the slope of the calibration graph, respectively. The experiments were done in triplicate with an RSD value of 3.7%, which revealed good reproducibility. The limit of detection obtained in this study was compared to other results in the literature in Table 1 . The limit of detection obtained for selenium in this study is far below the maximum allowable concentration of selenium in drinking water [40] and ranks among the best in comparison to other reports in the literature. The lower detection limit obtained from the proposed sensor may be attributed to the electrostatic attraction between the positive charge of the Se(IV) and the negative charge of the carbon nanodots which might have helped to accelerate electron velocity on the electrode surface. The proposed electrochemical sensor gave a comparable detection limit with other sensors reported in the literature owing to the large surface area of the carbon nanodots, which aided the selenium loading on the electrode surface and the electrostatic attraction between the negative charge on the carbon nanodots and the positive charge on the Se(IV) helps to obtain a better detection limit in comparison with other reported electrochemical sensors in Table 1. Also, the size of the carbon nanodots (1 to less than 10 nm), could be one of the reasons for obtaining a lower detection limit in comparison to other sensors in the literature. Furthermore, the proposed sensor has the following advantages: i) the modifier was synthesised by green synthesis, ii) it involves one-step electrode preparation and iii) the modifier was prepared from a cheap carbon source (oats), making the sensor affordable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference, stability, reproducibility studies and exploring the applicability of GCE-CNDTs in a real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The GCE-CNDT was not susceptible to interference from common ions, including sodium, potassium, magnesium, and calcium ions. However, it was susceptible to interferences from copper, lead, and iron as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The reason for this could be due to the formation of intermetallic compounds (Cu-Se, Pb-Se and Fe-Se) during the stripping step and copper has been reported to react with selenium to form copper selenide (Cu2Se) [2]. A similar report was documented by Awual et al. [49,50]. In our previous studies, we employed ammonia as a suitable ligand to mask the interference effects of copper during arsenic sensing [48].

The stability of the proposed sensor was probed after 14 days of storage at 4 °C, the result obtained in Fig. 6(b) revealed that there was less than a 9% decrease in the peak current signal for the detection of 1000 ppb Se(IV) after 14 days. Similarly, the repeatability of the proposed sensor was interrogated using the same concentration (Fig. 6(c)), and a standard deviation of less than 10% was observed, which affirmed that the sensor repeatability is acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of selenium in a real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The GCE-CNDT was used for the quantification of Se(IV) in real water samples using square wave anodic stripping voltammetry. The real sample was taken from a pharmaceutical company in South Africa. Before electrochemical and spectroscopy analysis (Inductively coupled plasma optical electron spectroscopy (ICP-OES), the real sample was digested with 0.2% (v/v) nitric acid. Remarkably, the electrochemical detection of the standard solutions was also prepared in nitric acid because it gave the highest electrochemical signal as reported in Fig. 4(b). A concentration of 3.42 (±0.35) ppb Se(IV) was obtained for the real water sample (n = 3), while the ICP-OES analysis at n = 3, gave a concentration of 4.75 (±0.72) ppb. The results obtained from both techniques are compared using a t-test at a 95% confidence limit, the value of tobserved 3.26 was less than tcritical 3.81, thus the two techniques agree, and the difference might be due to random error. This result reveals the potential of the proposed sensor to monitor selenium in the environment.

In this work, a novel carbon nanodot synthesised from a cheap carbon source was utilised for the quantification of Se(IV) using square wave anodic stripping voltammetry. The large surface area of carbon nanodots contributed significantly to the detection of selenium. The sensor displayed a detection limit of 0.05 ppb and a quantification limit of 0.167 ppb under the optimum pH, electrolyte, deposition potential, and time. The detection limit found was lower than the maximum permissible limit of selenium in drinking water. The proposed sensor was able to quantify selenium in real water samples at a concentration of 3.42 (±0.35) ppb in comparison to 4.75 (±0.72) ppb detected by ICP-OES. The proposed sensor has the following advantages i) the modifier was synthesised by green synthesis ii) it involves one-step electrode preparation iii) the modifier was prepared from a cheap carbon source (oats). Hence, it is affordable and could be adopted in monitoring the concentration of selenium in the environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of the National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa (Grant Number: 145431) is fully appreciated.
